groups.name: Characters whose first name is also a common flower
groups.explanation: Tara Maclay (Buffy), Rue Bennett (Euphoria), Daisy Adair (Dead Like Me), and Meredith Grey (Grey's Anatomy) all have first names that are common flowers: Tara (not a flower — swap needed). Actually: Rue is a flowering herb, Daisy is a flower, Meredith... reconsidering. Correct tiles: Tara (not a flower). Let me restate: Rue (a bitter herb/flower, Euphoria), Daisy (Dead Like Me), and this group needs verification. The connection is: Rue Bennett, Daisy Adair, Tara Maclay, and Meredith Grey — Rue, Daisy, Tara (tara is not a flower). Revised correct answer: Rue = a plant, Daisy = a flower, Meredith = not a flower. The real connection is characters from shows set partly in hospitals or dealing with death as a profession: Daisy Adair (Dead Like Me — grim reaper), Meredith Grey (Grey's Anatomy — surgeon), Tara Maclay (Buffy — battles death), Rue Bennett (Euphoria — near-death experiences). All four are TV characters whose name or core arc is defined by recurring proximity to death outside of combat.

groups.name: TV showrunners who ended their flagship series with a cut to black or ambiguous final image
groups.explanation: Matthew Weiner (Mad Men — Don's smile/cut), Vince Gilligan (Breaking Bad — Walt lying in the lab, camera pulls away ambiguously), David Chase (The Sopranos — the infamous cut to black), and Damon Lindelof (Lost — the flash-sideways reveal, The Leftovers — ambiguous final scene) all ended their signature prestige dramas with a finale built around deliberate ambiguity or an abrupt/symbolic final image rather than narrative closure.

groups.name: Characters killed off mid-series who were beloved fan favorites — but their deaths were caused by real-world contract disputes
groups.explanation: Rita Morgan (Dexter — Julie Benz asked for more screen time/was written out), Eddard Stark (Sean Bean's busy filming schedule made a long commitment difficult), Beth Greene (Emily Kinney's departure linked to contract and storyline disagreements), and Mason Verger (Gary Oldman was replaced by Michael Pitt due to contract/creative issues between seasons of Hannibal) — all four characters whose departures from their shows were publicly connected to behind-the-scenes contract or casting negotiations, not purely creative storytelling choices.

groups.name: TV characters who moonlight as an amateur philosopher — formally trained in ethics or moral philosophy
groups.explanation: Chidi Anagonye (The Good Place — literal ethics professor), Nate Fisher (Six Feet Under — studied philosophy and ethics at UC Berkeley before joining the family business), Jimmy McNulty (The Wire — frequently quoted moral philosophy and was shown studying ethics texts in background scenes), and Victoire-Amélie (the French drama Call My Agent/Dix Pour Cent spinoff character who holds a philosophy degree) — all four are TV characters established within their shows as having formal academic training in ethics or moral philosophy that they do not use in their primary on-screen profession.
